description: The provided image is unreadable as a generic UI icon and contains no discernible scientific data. Drawing from the supplied text extraction summary and paper context, this figure is understood to report X-ray diffraction patterns for three arc-cladded Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel samples designated Sample 1, Sample 2, and Sample 3. Sample 1 with composition Fe-0.45C-14Cr-1.53Mn-0.19Si is described as a single-phase Fe-Cr solid solution with peaks indexed as (110) near 45 degrees, (200) near 65 degrees, and (211) near 82 degrees. Samples 2 and 3 are described as single-phase Fe-Cr-Ni solid solutions. For all three samples, the diffraction peaks are shifted to higher two-theta relative to standard Fe, attributed to lattice distortion caused by atomic radius differences between Cr, Ni, and Fe. These XRD data support the conclusion that the arc-cladded coatings form complete solid solutions, consistent with calculated solid-solution parameters delta_r of 0.0335-0.0595 and Delta_H_mix from -1.213 to -0.451 kJ/mol, thereby validating the machine-learning framework for microhardness prediction with a minimum experimental error of 1.17 percent.

The figure presents a dual-panel visualization supporting a machine-learning investigation of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel microhardness. The left panel shows a dense matrix of small bivariate scatter or line plots labeled 'Data set,' representing a multivariate data matrix that visualizes pairwise relationships and distributions within the alloy dataset. Individual axis labels and numerical values in these sub-panels are unreadable at the given resolution, but the overall layout indicates exploratory data analysis of the high-dimensional input feature space. The right panel displays a funnel-shaped 'Importance ranking' diagram in which alloying elements are ranked by their influence on the target property: Cr (chromium) sits at the top as the most important predictor, followed sequentially by Ni (nickel) and Mo (molybdenum), with additional lower-ranked factors indicated by an ellipsis at the tapered bottom. This elemental ordering reflects data-driven correlation results identifying Cr, Ni, and Mo as the dominant positive contributors to microhardness. Taken together, the panels communicate both the structure of the training data and the resulting feature-importance hierarchy employed to guide inverse alloy design.

This figure presents machine-learning feature engineering and model optimization results for Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel microhardness prediction. The first composite panel contains three partially readable subplots: (a) a heatmap matrix with color-coded blocks likely representing prediction metrics or K-values across candidate features for hardness; (b) a correlation matrix displaying pairwise relationships among compositional or physical parameters via colored circles, where red and blue tones indicate positive and negative correlations respectively; and (c) a feature-importance bar chart comparing the relative contributions of selected descriptors. The second panel is a scatter plot of cross-validation score versus number of features ranging from 1 to 5, illustrating that model performance stabilizes or reaches an optimum at a low feature count, which aligns with caption-reported recursive feature elimination that identified an optimal three-feature combination yielding the lowest cross-validation error. Together, these plots support the core conclusion that rigorous, data-driven feature selection is essential for achieving extreme high-accuracy support-vector-regression-based hardness prediction and subsequent inverse alloy design.

A parity plot evaluating a support vector machine (SVM) regression model by comparing predicted versus experimental solid-solution strengthening increments (Δσ_SSS) for Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steels. The x-axis represents experimental Δσ_SSS from 1200 to 1800 MPa, and the y-axis represents predicted Δσ_SSS over the same range. Circular markers labeled 'All Samples' are color-mapped by prediction error using the right-hand color scale (ranging from roughly 0.60 to 29.98). A dashed diagonal guideline marks perfect prediction. Quantitative metrics displayed on the plot give RMSE = 0.31 and R² = 0.89. The close alignment of data points around the guideline indicates excellent predictive reliability. This result supports the paper's conclusion that integrating SVR with a modified solid solution strengthening theory—using the feature set {δ_Ec, D_Ec, G}—yields high-accuracy prediction of strengthening increments in arc-cladded alloys, improving upon the baseline performance (R² = 0.85, RMSE = 0.39).

The figure presents a bar chart comparing machine-learning-predicted and experimentally measured Vickers microhardness values for three arc-cladded Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel alloys labeled Sample 1, Sample 2, and Sample 3. The blue bars denote SVR-model predicted values, whereas the pink bars indicate experimental measurements, with error bars representing measurement variability and black percentage labels quantifying the prediction error for each alloy. Specifically, Sample 1 shows a predicted hardness of 521 HV against an experimental value of 515 HV (1.17 % error), Sample 2 registers 490 HV predicted versus 500 HV experimental (2.00 % error), and Sample 3 registers 415 HV predicted versus 424 HV experimental (2.09 % error). The close agreement across all three distinct compositions, highlighted by a minimum deviation of only 1.17 %, supports the core conclusion that the integrated machine learning framework—combining support vector regression, CGANs synthetic data augmentation, and firefly optimization—achieves extreme high accuracy in predicting and experimentally validating the microhardness of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si clad steels.

This figure presents a 12-by-12 correlation scatter matrix (pair plot) visualizing the multivariate relationships among eleven compositional variables—C, Si, Mn, Cr, S, Ni, P, Fe, Mo, V, and W (all in wt.%)—and Vickers microhardness (HV) for a dataset of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steels used in machine learning model training. The diagonal panels display kernel density estimate histograms revealing the distribution range and sampling frequency of each individual variable, whereas the off-diagonal panels contain scatter plots that expose pairwise linear or nonlinear correlations between elemental contents and the hardness response. Visually, microhardness shows a pronounced negative correlation with Fe content and strong positive correlations with C, Cr, Ni, Mo, V, and W, trends that align with solid-solution strengthening mechanisms. Additionally, certain element pairs such as Cr-Fe and Mo-Fe form characteristic inverse clusters. This exploratory visualization provides the foundational statistical evidence for feature importance ranking and supports the subsequent SVR-based microhardness prediction and firefly-algorithm-driven inverse alloy design.

Figure 3 presents a three-panel machine-learning feature-selection workflow for predicting the microhardness of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel. Panel (a) displays a recursive feature elimination (RFE) voting matrix across XGBoost, RF, and LightGBM, where dark blue cells (rank 1) highlight significant features including EI-2nd, eta_B, omega, delta_EI-2nd, D_Ec, delta_Ec, gamma, G, and e/a. Panel (b) is a Pearson correlation coefficient heatmap among these nine selected features, revealing extremely high correlations (absolute PCC equal to or greater than 0.95) between pairs such as EI-2nd and eta_B, omega and eta_B, EI-2nd and delta_EI-2nd, and D_Ec and delta_EI-2nd. Panel (c) ranks the importance scores of the nine features, showing EI-2nd as the dominant predictor with a score near 0.46, followed by eta_B, omega, and delta_EI-2nd. Based on this ranking, the lower-importance partners in highly correlated pairs were removed, ultimately identifying e/a, G, EI-2nd, delta_Ec, and D_Ec as the key features governing microhardness, which caption states are linked to solid-solution strengthening mechanisms.

Figure 4 presents the exhaustive feature-screening results for machine-learning prediction of microhardness in Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel. The scatter plot shows cross-validation scores on the y-axis against the number of selected features (1–5) on the x-axis. Using one feature yields the highest and most dispersed scores, ranging from about 0.3 to above 1.2. Increasing to two features markedly lowers the scores to roughly 0.25–0.7. With three features, the scores drop further and cluster tightly around 0.25–0.35, aligning with the reported optimal three-feature set {δ_Ec, D_Ec, G} that gives the lowest cross-validation RMSE of 0.257. At four and five features, the scores remain low and stable near 0.25–0.30, indicating diminishing returns. These trends demonstrate that a concise three-feature combination derived from solid-solution-strengthening theory delivers the best predictive performance for the support-vector-regression model without overfitting.

Figure 5 comprises nine scatter subplots (a–i) comparing predicted versus experimental microhardness (HV) for Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel using nine machine learning regression models: SVR (a), ET (b), MLP (c), Ridge (d), LightGBM (e), XGBoost (f), RF (g), LR (h), and DT (i). Each subplot displays training data (blue circles) and testing data (yellow circles) against a 1:1 dashed diagonal line over the range 300–800 HV. The caption states that three features {G, δ_Ec, D_Ec} served as inputs for microhardness prediction. Quantitative annotations show SVR achieves the best performance with R² = 0.85 and RMSE = 0.39, followed by Ridge (R² = 0.82, RMSE = 0.43), MLP (R² = 0.81, RMSE = 0.44), and ET (R² = 0.80, RMSE = 0.45), whereas LightGBM yields the lowest accuracy (R² = 0.63, RMSE = 0.62). The accompanying text reports that ET, XGBoost, and DT exhibit potential overfitting based on their testing data scatter. Overall, the figure establishes SVR as the optimal model for microhardness prediction in this alloy system.

Figure 6 presents a parity plot comparing machine-learning-predicted solid solution strengthening increments (Δσ_SSS, in MPa) against experimentally measured values for Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel under ten-fold cross-validation. Each scatter point represents an individual alloy sample, with the color scale on the right indicating the absolute prediction error ranging from 0.60 to 29.90 MPa. A dashed blue guide line marks the ideal one-to-one correspondence between predicted and experimental data. The plot quantifies model accuracy with an R² of 0.89 and an RMSE of 0.31. According to the caption, the predictive model uses {δ_Ec, D, G} as input features. The samples predominantly span the 1300–1700 MPa range and distribute tightly along the guide line, visually confirming that the coupled SVR and modified solid-solution-strengthening theory accurately captures the solid-solution hardening contribution across the investigated alloy compositions.

Figure 7 presents a bar chart comparing the predicted microhardness of inverse-designed Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel obtained using four intelligent optimization algorithms. The x-axis lists the algorithms—Firefly Algorithm (FA), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Simulated Annealing (SA), and Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO)—while the y-axis reports microhardness in Vickers Hardness (HV). The FA algorithm yields the highest predicted value at 733.59 HV, followed closely by PSO at 730.04 HV, then GWO at 708.60 HV, with SA producing the lowest value at 700.37 HV. This ranking indicates that FA possesses superior local attraction capacity for this materials design problem, consistent with its generalized form relationship to SA, PSO, and differential evolution. The results are part of a machine learning framework combining support vector regression and conditional generative adversarial networks to predict and optimize alloy hardness. The figure substantiates the selection of FA as the preferred optimization strategy for inverse designing high-hardness Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel compositions, directly guiding subsequent experimental validation through arc cladding.

Figure 8 comprises X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns characterizing three arc-cladded Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel samples designated Sample 1, Sample 2, and Sample 3. Panel (a) displays the full angular range from 2θ = 20° to 100°, revealing principal Bragg reflections indexed as (110), (200), and (211). The top spectrum (Sample 1) is labeled as the Fe-Cr phase, while the middle (Sample 2) and bottom (Sample 3) spectra are labeled as the Fe-Cr-Ni phase. Panel (b) magnifies the strongest (110) peak within the 40°–50° window, with a dashed vertical line marking the standard Fe position near 44°. The alloy peaks are systematically shifted to higher diffraction angles compared with pure Fe, with Sample 1 exhibiting the largest offset and Sample 3 a smaller offset. Caption states this rightward peak shift originates from lattice distortion caused by atomic radius differences between Cr, Ni and Fe, thereby supporting the formation of substitutional solid solutions in the machine-learning-designed alloys.

Figure 9 presents a grouped bar chart comparing the machine-learning-predicted and experimental Vickers microhardness values for three Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si alloy samples. The blue bars denote predicted values and the red bars denote experimental values. Sample 1 exhibits a predicted hardness of 521 HV and an experimental value of 515 HV, corresponding to a 1.17% relative error. Sample 2 shows a predicted value of 490 HV against an experimental value of 500 HV (2.00% error), and Sample 3 shows 415 HV predicted versus 424 HV experimental (2.09% error). Error bars indicate measurement uncertainty on each data point. The strong agreement between predicted and experimental columns across all three samples, with maximum deviation under 2.1%, demonstrates the high fidelity of the predictive model. This figure provides direct experimental evidence validating the extreme high accuracy of the machine learning approach for microhardness prediction in these alloys.
